title: Chapter 6 Quiz
description: Test your knowledge of Arrays, Dictionaries, and Sets!

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of a Collection (like an Array)?
options: To group multiple pieces of data into a single variable.
To hide data from the user.
questions.explanation: Collections allow you to bundle 5, 50, or 5000 individual pieces of data under a single variable name.

questions.question: What brackets are used to create an Array?
questions.explanation: Arrays, Dictionaries, and Sets all primarily use square brackets `[ ]` for their data.

questions.question: In Swift, an Array can hold Strings and Integers at the exact same time (e.g. `["Tim", 50]`).
questions.explanation: Collections in Swift must be strictly typed. An array can hold Strings OR Integers, but never securely mixed together by default.

questions.question: In programming, counting the position (Index) of an item in an Array always starts at the number _____.
questions.explanation: Zero-based indexing is a core rule of programming. Item 1 is at index 0.

questions.question: If `var cars = ["Ford", "BMW", "Audi"]`, what does `cars[1]` output?
questions.explanation: Because counting starts at 0, index 1 corresponds to the SECOND item: 'BMW'.

questions.question: What two pieces of data does a Dictionary hold together?
questions.explanation: A Dictionary links a Key (used for lookup) specifically to a Value (the data you want).

questions.question: How do you separate a Key from its Value when building a Dictionary?
questions.explanation: Dictionaries are defined as `["Key": "Value"]`.

questions.question: When you look up a value in a dictionary (e.g., `dict["Name"]`), the data that comes back is always safely wrapped as an Optional.
questions.explanation: Because the Key might not exist in the dictionary, Swift purposefully protects you from a crash by returning an Optional (which might be `nil`).

questions.question: Which of these collections completely forbids duplicate data?
questions.explanation: Sets guarantee exclusivity. Every single item inside a Set is guaranteed to be 100% unique.

questions.question: If you query a Set using the `._____()` function, it returns a blazing-fast True or False regarding whether an item exists inside it.
questions.explanation: The `.contains("Item")` method is the primary mathematical reason Sets are unbelievably fast at lookups.

questions.question: Why would you choose an Array instead of a Set?
options: Because you want the data to be ordered.
Because you want to allow duplicate entries.
Because you want to retrieve data by index (e.g., array[0]).
questions.explanation: Arrays maintain their order and allow duplicates. Sets ruin order and destroy duplicates.

questions.question: What code do you use to easily process every item inside an Array?
questions.explanation: A `for-in` loop effortlessly runs a block of code for every single item inside a collection.

questions.question: When querying a Dictionary `for (a, b) in userNames`, what does `a` represent?
questions.explanation: A Dictionary iterates using a grouping called a Tuple. It is ALWAYS returned in the order `(Key, Value)`.

questions.question: How do you add a brand new item to the END of an Array?
questions.explanation: The `.append()` function is the correct Swift method to add an item to the end of an Array.

questions.question: If `var scores = [10, 20]`, what happens if you try to `print(scores[5])`?
options: The app crashes with an 'Index out of range' error.
questions.explanation: Unlike looking up a missing string in a Dictionary (which returns nil), trying to access an out-of-bounds Index in an Array instantly crashes your app!

questions.question: How do you set a new key-value pair in a Dictionary?
questions.explanation: You assign the Value to the Dictionary using the square-bracket Key lookup: `dict["NewKey"] = NewValue`.

questions.question: Because a Set scrambles the order of contents, if you iterate over it with a for loop, it will NOT print in the order you added the items.
questions.explanation: Sets are completely unordered collections. Iterating over them will give you data in seemingly random order.

questions.question: If you must explicitly tell Swift you are creating a Set, which syntax is correct?
questions.explanation: Swift syntax for explicit generic typing uses angle-brackets: `Set<Type>`.

questions.question: You can find out how many items are currently stored inside an Array by typing `array._____`.
questions.explanation: The `.count` property returns an Int detailing how many items exist in any collection.

questions.question: You can declare a completely empty Array of Strings by writing `var list = _____`.
questions.explanation: To initialize an empty array of a specific type, you use the type inside brackets followed by parentheses: `[String]()`.
